TextENGNew: Summer, 2006.
"...Mmm~!@k@vS10/40/430100002. I'd heard the stories, but it really is a beautiful view~."
Standing at the crest of the hill, I stretched my arms wide and drew in a long, deep breath, letting the crisp air fill me completely.
Spread out before me was a tranquil pastoral landscape.

The gassho-zukuri farmhouses, with their distinctive old thatched roofs, dotted the landscape as if they had grown there naturally — beautiful, almost seamlessly part of the earth. Far more breathtaking than I had imagined, it drew an involuntary sigh from my lips.
changeReason: New preserves the specific architectural term 合掌造り ('gassho-zukuri') and captures 溶け込むように ('as if blending in naturally'), both of which the old translation omits.

TextENGNew: "We could have started here together, Dad.@k@vS10/40/430100004. ...You didn't need to say it would be more efficient to split up."
Saying that, I turned my gaze in the direction where my father was probably working.
Of course there was no way to see him from here, but the image that sprang to mind — of him hunched over his documents with a furrowed brow — made me burst out laughing.
If I recalled correctly, my father was currently investigating the swamp thought to be the source of the volcanic gas.
changeReason: New includes 確か ('if I recall correctly') which the old omits, and more precisely conveys that the father is 'investigating' rather than merely 'heading off to survey.'

TextENGNew: ...It was called the Onigafuchi Swamp, I believed.
These days the area had been filled in with concrete, leaving only a wide, empty clearing in the middle of the forest.
"...Well, then.@k@vS10/40/430100006. I suppose I should start with the standard approach — taking photographs first."
Bidding farewell to the beautiful view, I retraced my steps back the way I had come.
The air was thick with the green smell of grass; lush, vibrant growth surrounded me on all sides.
changeReason: New correctly translates 草いきれ as the thick, steamy smell of grass in the air, which is a specific Japanese term for the oppressive verdant atmosphere; old omits this sensory detail entirely.

TextENGNew: Yesterday's raindrops still clung to everything, catching the sun and sparkling brilliantly.
When I stepped on the soft grass, my feet sank in up to my ankles and the hidden water oozed slowly up around them.
changeReason: Old says water 'began pouring in' but the Japanese (じわり、と浸み出してきた) describes water slowly seeping/oozing out — a gradual, gentle process rather than pouring.

TextENGNew: In a small clearing, I spotted a stone lantern, blackened with grime.
There had once been lawns and cultivated plantings here, no doubt — now a lone pine stood among the ranks of cedar, quietly growing where it had been left.
changeReason: Old reverses the figure/ground: JPN describes a lone pine among ranks of cedar, but old describes clusters of cedar against pines — swapped which tree dominates the scene.

TextENGNew: ...The shrine grounds too, now entirely swallowed by tall weeds, looked little different from any other flat patch of remote mountainside.
changeReason: New correctly translates 境内 as 'shrine grounds,' providing the specific setting the old translation generalizes as simply 'the area.'

TextENGNew: Continuing to take photographs on my way toward the main hall, I stopped in front of the ritual storehouse.
The bolt had been broken; through the gap of the slightly ajar door, I could catch a glimpse of the dark interior.
According to the materials I had researched beforehand, the storehouse held an assortment of deeply unsettling implements and tools...
"I wonder... is it all right to take photographs here?@k@vS10/40/430100010. What if I get struck down for it?@k@vS10/40/430100011. ...Yeah, better not."
After a moment's hesitation, I decided to leave it for later and moved on.
changeReason: New correctly translates 迷って as 'hesitation' (not 'getting lost in thought') and 後回しにしよう as 'leave it for later' (not merely 'leave the area'), capturing the speaker's decision to postpone rather than abandon.

TextENGNew: I had gone inside once already for a quick look — but the thought of going in again, alone, was more than I could manage.
"Still... it's remarkable how quickly buildings fall to ruin once people leave them.@k@vS10/40/430100013. I suppose a shrine with no one to tend it is much the same."
I stood looking directly at the main hall and found myself sighing.
The paint had been stripped bare by years of wind and rain, the decorative metal fittings were rusted through, and the wooden parts — well, one need hardly say more.
"...Right then.@k@vS10/40/430100015. Where should I head next?"
I settled myself down under the torii gate, pulled out the village map I'd tucked into my breast pocket, and spread it open.
changeReason: New preserves the specific term 鳥居 ('torii gate') while old generalizes it to 'shrine gate'; the torii is a distinct and culturally significant architectural element.

TextENGNew: It was my own creation — a CG map I had built up from the painstaking surveys my father and his detective colleagues had carried out.

I was rather proud of how it had turned out, and my father — hopeless with technology as he is — had apparently been impressed enough to say, the first time he saw it, "You picked the wrong line of work. You'd make far more money at a design firm."
Not that I ever wished he had said something like that sooner. Not even slightly.
Because — however grandiose it might sound — I truly believed I was born to be a detective.
And it was to carry on my father's will that I had come to this village.
"Let's see, I'm at the Furude Shrine right now, so..."
I looked up at the sun riding high in the sky to confirm my bearings, then dropped my gaze to the map spread before me and drew out a ballpoint pen from my breast pocket.
